interest: Multiple stakeholders — equity advocates, employers, the reform-realist government, and independent researchers — share an interest in whether S1's redistributive core (SES weighting) can actually function administratively rather than existing only on paper.
value: Equity (real redistribution toward low-SES applicants) is in tension with administrative feasibility and gaming-resistance; a proxy that can be gamed converts an equity instrument into a legitimising fiction.
fear: The driving fear is that S1 gets adopted as 'reform' while the SES quota collapses into a gameable or unenforceable mechanism, leaving low-SES pupils no better off but the system relabelled as fixed — which advocates explicitly call worse than no promise.
affected: high-SES families (who could game a self-reported proxy)
assumption: The claim rests on the assumption that no administratively workable, gaming-resistant SES proxy can be built without a student-level SES register — i.e. that register linkage (KSH/Oktatási Hivatal) either cannot be constructed or would still be gameable.
empirical_uncertainty: The factual core is contested-to-unknown: it is established that Hungary has no student-level SES register and that self-reported proxies are gameable, but whether a linked administrative proxy (tax/child-benefit registers) could be built to be workable and gaming-resistant is explicitly unresolved and named as the object of a feasibility study by several voices.
